hook: You already know how to calculate the mean of a sample — but how confident can you really be that it reflects the true population mean? A single number rarely tells the whole story, and in real life, decisions in medicine, business, and policy depend on knowing *how close* our estimates actually are. In this unit, you'll discover how statisticians turn uncertainty into a precise, honest statement about what the data is telling us.
concept: The unifying idea is that we can use sample data to make informed, quantified guesses about unknown population parameters — not just point estimates, but ranges that carry a measurable level of confidence, formally captured in the idea of an *interval estimator with a stated confidence level*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn the difference between a point estimate and an interval estimate, and why acknowledging uncertainty makes statistical conclusions more honest and useful.
Master the step-by-step process of constructing a confidence interval for a population mean $\mu$ using the $z$- or $t$-distribution, depending on what you know about the population.
Apply the confidence interval framework to categorical data by estimating an unknown population proportion $p$ from a sample percentage.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Margin of Error and Sample Size
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn how to control the precision of your estimates by calculating the margin of error and determining the minimum sample size needed to achieve a desired level of accuracy.
Master the careful, correct interpretation of what a confidence interval does — and does not — say, avoiding the most common misconceptions that trip up even experienced readers of statistics.
